questions.question: What is the most common malignant neoplasm that originates in the mucosa of the large intestine?
questions.explanation: Colorectal cancer is defined as a malignant neoplasm originating in the mucosa of the large intestine, and approximately 95% of all CRCs are adenocarcinomas, developing from adenomatous polyps.

questions.question: Which non-modifiable risk factor is the most significant for colorectal cancer?
questions.explanation: Advanced age is the most significant non-modifiable risk factor for colorectal cancer, with incidence increasing dramatically after age 50 years.

questions.question: What genetic syndrome is associated with mutations in the APC gene and hundreds of adenomatous polyps?
questions.explanation: Familial Adenomatous Polyposis (FAP) is a high-risk hereditary syndrome caused by mutations in the APC gene, characterized by hundreds or thousands of adenomatous polyps in the colon and rectum, with a risk of CRC close to 100% if untreated.

questions.question: What is the predominant molecular pathway in sporadic colorectal carcinogenesis?
questions.explanation: The Chromosome Instability Pathway (CIN) or Classical Pathway is the predominant one, representing approximately 85% of sporadic colorectal cancers, characterized by aneuploidy and multiple chromosomal rearrangements.

questions.question: Which mutation is the earliest initiating genetic event in the classical CRC pathway?
questions.explanation: Mutations in the APC (Adenomatous Polyposis Coli) gene are the earliest initiating event in the pathway of chromosomal instability, affecting approximately 80% of sporadic cases and leading to the formation of early adenomas.

questions.question: What is the most common site of distant metastasis in colorectal cancer?
questions.explanation: Due to portal venous drainage of the colon, the liver is the most common site of distant metastasis in colorectal cancer, affecting 60-70% of patients with metastatic disease.

questions.question: What position does CRC occupy as a cause of cancer death worldwide?
questions.explanation: Colorectal cancer is the second leading cause of cancer death worldwide, underscoring its significant burden in morbidity and mortality.

questions.question: What is the most common and earliest symptom of right colon cancer?
questions.explanation: Tumors of the right colon usually bleed chronically and insidiously, leading to slow blood loss that manifests as iron deficiency anemia, being the most common symptom.

questions.question: What symptom is more common in left colon cancer due to the smaller intestinal diameter?
questions.explanation: In left colon cancer, changes in bowel habit (new constipation, diarrhea or alternation) are more frequent and noticeable due to the smaller diameter of the intestinal lumen and the more solid consistency of the stool.

questions.question: What finding on the physical examination is essential for the diagnosis and staging of rectal cancer?
questions.explanation: Rectal examination is an essential maneuver for the diagnosis and staging of cancer of the rectum and anal canal, allowing masses to be palpated, evaluate their size, consistency and fixation, as well as the involvement of adjacent structures.

questions.question: What is the test considered 'gold standard' for CRC screening?
questions.explanation: Colonoscopy is considered the gold standard for colorectal cancer screening, since it allows you to visualize the entire colon, identify lesions, and perform biopsies or polypectomies in the same procedure.

questions.question: What tumor marker is used primarily for CRC monitoring, not for initial diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Carcinoembryonic Antigen (CEA) is the main tumor marker in CRC, but it is not used for screening or initial diagnosis due to its low sensitivity and specificity. Its usefulness lies in post-treatment monitoring to detect recurrence and evaluate the response to treatment.

questions.question: What imaging study is essential for local staging of rectal cancer?
questions.explanation: Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) of the pelvis is essential for the local staging of rectal cancer, providing excellent soft tissue resolution to evaluate the depth of invasion in the rectal wall, the relationship with the mesorectum and the involvement of regional lymph nodes.

questions.question: What does the 'T1' category mean in the TNM classification of colorectal cancer?
questions.explanation: In the TNM classification, T1 indicates that the tumor invades the submucosa, which represents an early stage of invasion into the intestinal wall.

questions.question: What does N1a indicate in the TNM classification for lymph nodes?
questions.explanation: N1a in the TNM classification means that there is metastasis in 1 regional lymph node, which denotes limited nodal involvement.

questions.question: What describes stage IV colorectal cancer?
questions.explanation: Stage IV colorectal cancer is defined by the presence of distant metastases (M1), regardless of the extent of the primary tumor (T) or lymph node involvement (N).

questions.question: What is the most common histological type of colorectal cancer?
questions.explanation: Adenocarcinoma (NOS - Not Otherwise Specified) represents 90-95% of colorectal cancers, being the most common histological type.

questions.question: What condition can mimic a colorectal tumor due to inflammatory masses?
questions.explanation: Amebiasis, a parasitic infection, can cause the formation of amebomas, which are inflammatory masses in the colon that can mimic a malignant tumor on imaging studies.

questions.question: Which inflammatory bowel disease significantly increases the long-term risk of CRC?
questions.explanation: Long-standing ulcerative colitis, especially with extensive colon involvement, significantly increases the risk of dysplasia and colorectal cancer due to chronic inflammation and accelerated cell regeneration.

questions.question: What is the main surgery for colon cancer, including lymph nodes?
questions.explanation: Colectomy is the main surgery for colon cancer, which involves the resection of the affected colon segment along with its vessels and the mesocolon containing the lymph nodes (lymphadenectomy), with the aim of resecting the primary tumor and regional nodes.

questions.question: What adjuvant chemotherapy regimen is common for stage III CRC?
questions.explanation: For Stage III CRC, common adjuvant chemotherapy regimens include FOLFOX (5-Fluorouracil, Leucovorin, Oxaliplatin) or CapeOx (Capecitabine, Oxaliplatin), usually administered for 3 to 6 months to eliminate micrometastases and reduce the risk of recurrence.

questions.question: What targeted therapy is used in metastatic CRC with KRAS/NRAS wild-type?
questions.explanation: Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors such as Cetuximab or Panitumumab are effective only in tumors with KRAS and NRAS wild-type (not mutated), since the mutations confer resistance to these therapies.

questions.question: For which type of metastatic CRC is immunotherapy with PD-1 inhibitors indicated?
questions.explanation: PD-1 inhibitors (Pembrolizumab, Nivolumab) are indicated for patients with metastatic CRC with microsatellite instability high (MSI-H) or DNA mismatch repair system (dMMR) deficiency, as these tumors are very sensitive to immunotherapy.

questions.question: What type of diet is recommended for a patient before surgery or invasive procedure?
questions.explanation: For a patient who is going to undergo invasive procedures or surgery, NPO (Nothing Oral) is usually indicated starting at 10:00 p.m. the day before to guarantee gastric emptying and reduce aspiration risks.

questions.question: Which laboratory test is crucial to detect anemia in CRC?
questions.explanation: A complete blood count (CBC) is crucial as it can reveal iron deficiency (microcytic, hypochromic) anemia, a common finding in right colon tumors due to chronic bleeding.

questions.question: What is the adenoma-carcinoma sequence in the development of CRC?
options: Transformation of premalignant polyp to invasive carcinoma
questions.explanation: The adenoma-carcinoma sequence is the process by which premalignant lesions (adenomatous or serrated polyps) gradually transform, through an accumulation of genetic and epigenetic alterations, into an invasive carcinoma.

questions.question: What percentage of CRC is associated with hereditary genetic syndromes?
questions.explanation: Family history and genetic syndromes account for approximately 5-10% of all colorectal cancers, including high-risk syndromes such as FAP and Lynch Syndrome.

questions.question: What lifestyle factor is associated with an increased risk of CRC?
questions.explanation: Physical inactivity or sedentary lifestyle is an independent modifiable risk factor that is associated with an increased risk of developing colorectal cancer.

questions.question: Which mutated tumor suppressor gene is associated with late stages of CRC progression?
questions.explanation: Mutations in the TP53 gene (present in 50-70% of CRC) occur in late stages of tumor progression in the chromosomal instability pathway, allowing the accumulation of more mutations and complete malignant transformation.

questions.question: What gene is commonly mutated in the serrated pathway of colorectal carcinogenesis?
questions.explanation: Mutations in BRAF (V600E) are very common in the serrated pathway (present in ~50% of SSPs and ~70% of CRCs originating in this pathway), activating the RAS-MAPK pathway and promoting cell proliferation.

questions.question: What is the current trend in the incidence of CRC in young people?
questions.explanation: There is a worrying trend of increasing incidence of colorectal cancer in young people (under 50 years of age), especially in Western countries, a phenomenon that is not yet fully understood.

questions.question: What term describes the constant feeling of needing to defecate in rectal cancer?
questions.explanation: Tenesmus is the constant feeling of needing to defecate, even after having done so, due to irritation or mass in the rectum, and is a common symptom in rectal cancer.

questions.question: What sign of distant metastasis can be observed in Virchow's lymph node?
questions.explanation: Palpation of supraclavicular lymphadenopathy, especially the Virchow lymph node (left supraclavicular), is a sign suggestive of distant metastasis on physical examination.

questions.question: What type of chemotherapy is given before surgery to shrink the tumor?
questions.explanation: Neoadjuvant chemotherapy is administered before surgery, mainly in locally advanced rectal cancer, with the aim of reducing the size of the tumor, facilitating a complete resection and reducing the risk of local recurrence.

questions.question: What is the main purpose of neoadjuvant radiotherapy in rectal cancer?
options: Reduce tumor size and local recurrence
questions.explanation: Neoadjuvant radiotherapy in locally advanced rectal cancer has the main objective of reducing the size of the tumor, which facilitates a complete surgical resection, and significantly reducing the risk of local recurrence.

questions.question: What molecular analysis is essential to select anti-EGFR therapies in metastatic CRC?
questions.explanation: Mutations in the KRAS and NRAS genes are essential to select patients who will benefit from anti-EGFR therapies (such as cetuximab or panitumumab), since these drugs are only effective in 'wild-type' tumors for KRAS/NRAS.

questions.question: A malignant neoplasm that originates in the mucosa of the large intestine, commonly an adenocarcinoma, is defined as:
questions.explanation: Colorectal cancer is defined as a malignant neoplasm originating in the mucosa of the large intestine, the vast majority being adenocarcinomas that evolve from pre-existing polyps, through the adenoma-carcinoma sequence.

questions.question: What is the most significant non-modifiable risk factor for colorectal cancer?
questions.explanation: Advanced age is the most significant non-modifiable risk factor for colorectal cancer, with incidence increasing dramatically after age 50 years. Most diagnoses occur in people over 60-70 years old.

questions.question: What genetic syndrome is characterized by mutations in the APC gene and hundreds of adenomatous polyps?
questions.explanation: Familial Adenomatous Polyposis (FAP) is caused by mutations in the APC gene and is characterized by the presence of hundreds or thousands of adenomatous polyps in the colon and rectum, with a risk of cancer close to 100% if untreated.

questions.question: What is a modifiable dietary risk factor for CRC?
questions.explanation: High consumption of red and processed meats is a well-established modifiable dietary risk factor for colorectal cancer, associated with the formation of carcinogens during cooking at high temperatures.

questions.question: What modifiable lifestyle factor increases the risk of CRC?
questions.explanation: Physical inactivity or sedentary lifestyle is an independent and modifiable risk factor for the development of colorectal cancer, as are obesity and smoking.

questions.question: Which tumor suppressor gene is the earliest initiating event in the chromosomal instability pathway?
questions.explanation: Mutations in the APC (Adenomatous Polyposis Coli) gene are the earliest initiating event in approximately 80% of sporadic cases of colorectal cancer, leading to the formation of early adenomas.

questions.question: What mutation occurs in 40% of CRC and confers resistance to anti-EGFR therapies?
questions.explanation: Activating mutations in the KRAS gene occur in approximately 40% of colorectal cancers. These mutations promote adenoma progression and confer resistance to anti-EGFR therapies, which is crucial for treatment selection.

questions.question: Which gene, known as the 'guardian of the genome', is mutated in late stages of tumor progression?
questions.explanation: The TP53 gene, known as the 'guardian of the genome', induces cell cycle arrest or apoptosis in response to DNA damage. Its mutations, present in 50-70% of CRC, occur in late stages of tumor progression, allowing the accumulation of more mutations and complete malignant transformation.

questions.question: What genes are commonly mutated in Lynch Syndrome (microsatellite instability pathway)?
questions.explanation: Lynch Syndrome (Hereditary Nonpolyposis Colorectal Cancer) is characterized by inherited germline mutations in the genes of the DNA mismatch repair (MMR) system, specifically MLH1, MSH2, MSH6 and PMS2, leading to microsatellite instability.

questions.question: What epigenetic event can silence MLH1 and cause microsatellite instability in sporadic CRC?
questions.explanation: In 10-15% of sporadic CRC, hypermethylation of the MLH1 gene promoter silences its expression, leading to MLH1 deficiency and the microsatellite instability (MSI) phenotype, characteristic of the serrated pathway and some sporadic tumors.

questions.question: Which mutation is very common in the serrated pathway of colorectal carcinogenesis?
questions.explanation: Mutations in BRAF (V600E) are very common in the serrated pathway of colorectal carcinogenesis, present in approximately 50% of sessile serrated polyps (SSP) and 70% of CRCs originated by this pathway, activating the RAS-MAPK pathway.

questions.question: What is the most common site of distant metastasis from colorectal cancer?
questions.explanation: The liver is the most common site of distant metastasis from colorectal cancer, affecting 60-70% of patients with metastatic disease. This is due to portal venous drainage of the colon.

questions.question: What position does CRC occupy in cancer mortality worldwide?
questions.explanation: Colorectal cancer is the second leading cause of cancer death globally, underscoring its significant impact on public health and the need for prevention and early detection strategies.

questions.question: What is the worrying trend in the age of CRC diagnosis?
options: Increase in those under 50 years of age
questions.explanation: There is a worrying trend of increasing incidence of colorectal cancer in young people (under 50 years of age), known as early-onset CRC, the cause of which is still being investigated, but is related to changes in diet and lifestyle.

questions.question: How do screening programs impact CRC?
questions.explanation: Screening programs (colonoscopy, fecal occult blood testing) have been shown to be highly effective in reducing the incidence and mortality of colorectal cancer, by detecting and removing precancerous polyps and diagnosing cancers at early stages.

questions.question: What is the most common symptom of right colon cancer?
questions.explanation: Iron deficiency anemia is the most common symptom and often the first manifestation of right colon cancer, due to chronic and insidious bleeding that causes slow and progressive blood loss, detectable by laboratory analysis.

questions.question: Which symptom is more common in left colon cancer due to the smaller diameter of the lumen?
questions.explanation: Changes in bowel habit, such as new-onset constipation or alternation between diarrhea and constipation, are more frequent and noticeable in left colon cancer due to the smaller diameter of the intestinal lumen and the more solid consistency of the stool.

questions.question: What symptom is the most common of rectal cancer?
questions.explanation: Rectal bleeding, defined as bright red blood or clots, often on the toilet paper or dripping into the bowl after defecation, is the most common symptom of rectal cancer. Although it is often confused with hemorrhoids, it requires investigation.

questions.question: Which systemic symptom suggests advanced disease or metastasis in CRC?
questions.explanation: Unexplained weight loss is a major red flag symptom in colorectal cancer that suggests more advanced disease, possibly with metastasis, and is associated with tumor burden and body catabolism.

questions.question: Which finding on physical examination suggests intestinal obstruction in a patient with CRC?
questions.explanation: Generalized bloating in the abdomen suggests distention of intestinal loops, which is common in intestinal obstruction, a significant and often late complication of colorectal cancer.

questions.question: What maneuver is essential for the diagnosis and staging of rectal cancer?
questions.explanation: Rectal examination is an essential maneuver for the diagnosis and staging of rectal and anal canal cancer. It allows evaluating the presence, size, consistency and mobility of rectal masses, as well as their relationship with adjacent structures and the cul-de-sac of Douglas.

questions.question: What is the 'gold standard' for colorectal cancer screening?
questions.explanation: Colonoscopy is considered the 'gold standard' for colorectal cancer screening, as it allows direct visualization of the entire colon and rectum, identification of polyps and lesions, and performance of biopsies or polypectomies in the same procedure.

questions.question: Which screening test detects non-visible blood in stool and is preferable because of its sensitivity?
questions.explanation: The fecal immunochemical test (FIT) is preferable over the traditional fecal occult blood test due to its greater sensitivity and specificity to detect non-visible human blood in feces, without requiring dietary restrictions, being an effective option for annual screening.

questions.question: What is the main use of Carcinoembryonic Antigen (CEA) in CRC?
questions.explanation: The main use of Carcinoembryonic Antigen (CEA) in colorectal cancer is post-treatment follow-up to detect recurrence and evaluation of the response to treatment in advanced disease, since it is not used for screening or initial diagnosis due to its low sensitivity and specificity.

questions.question: What imaging test is most used for staging disseminated disease?
options: Computed Tomography (CT) of the chest, abdomen and pelvis
questions.explanation: Computed Tomography (CT) of the chest, abdomen and pelvis is the most used imaging test for the staging of colorectal cancer, allowing the evaluation of local extension, the presence of regional lymphadenopathy and, crucially, the detection of distant metastases in organs such as the liver and lungs.

questions.question: What imaging study is essential for local staging of rectal cancer?
questions.explanation: Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) of the pelvis is essential for local staging of rectal cancer. It provides excellent resolution of soft tissues, evaluating the depth of invasion in the rectal wall, the relationship with the mesorectum and lymph node involvement, critical information for surgical planning and neoadjuvant care.

questions.question: What mutations are necessary to select patients who will benefit from anti-EGFR therapies?
questions.explanation: KRAS and NRAS mutations are necessary to select patients who will benefit from anti-EGFR therapies (such as Cetuximab or Panitumumab) in metastatic colorectal cancer, since these drugs are only effective in 'wild-type' tumors (not mutated) for these genes.

questions.question: According to the TNM classification, a tumor that invades the submucosa is designated as:
questions.explanation: According to the AJCC TNM classification, a tumor that invades the submucosa is designated as T1. Tis corresponds to carcinoma in situ, T2 to invasion of the muscularis propria and T3 to invasion through the muscularis propria.

questions.question: In TNM staging, an N1b indicates metastasis in:
options: Tumor deposits without lymph node involvement
questions.explanation: In the AJCC TNM staging system, N1b indicates metastases in 2 to 3 regional lymph nodes, while N1a is 1 node, N1c is tumor deposits without nodal involvement, and N2a is 4 to 6 nodes.

questions.question: A patient with metastasis to a single distant organ (M1a) has a stage:
questions.explanation: According to the AJCC Stage Group classification, the presence of distant metastases classifies the disease as Stage IV. Specifically, M1a (metastasis to a single organ or distant site) corresponds to Stage IVA, while M1b and M1c are Stages IVB and IVC respectively.

questions.question: Which variant of colorectal adenocarcinoma is associated with MSI and worse response to chemotherapy?
questions.explanation: Mucinous adenocarcinoma is characterized by having more than 50% of the tumor mass composed of extracellular mucin. It is frequently associated with microsatellite instability (MSI) and may have a worse response to certain chemotherapy regimens in some settings, influencing therapeutic decisions.

questions.question: Which molecular profile is a primary biomarker for checkpoint inhibitor immunotherapy?
questions.explanation: Microsatellite instability high (MSI-H) or deficiency of the DNA mismatch repair system (dMMR) is the main biomarker for immunotherapy with checkpoint inhibitors. MSI-H/dMMR tumors have a high mutational load and are very sensitive to these treatments, achieving durable responses.

questions.question: What type of colectomy is performed for right colon tumors?
questions.explanation: For tumors located in the right colon (cecum, ascending colon, hepatic flexure), the standard surgical intervention is right hemicolectomy, which involves resection of the affected segment along with its vessels and regional lymph nodes.

questions.question: What surgical technique is essential in rectal cancer to reduce local recurrence?
questions.explanation: Total mesorectal resection (TRM) is a fundamental surgical technique for rectal cancer, either by low anterior resection (LAR) or abdominoperineal resection (APR). Its goal is to remove the tumor along with all lymphatic and fatty tissue (mesorectum) around the rectum, drastically reducing the rate of local recurrence.
